analogy: Think of "Mineral and Power Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: Social and Political Life 28 Unit Two 2024-25 29 Teacher’s Note This is a continuation of the theme of government that has already been dealt with in the Class VI and VII textbooks. Hence, a recap of ideas is important especially those related to elections,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: AJSU Party (AJSU) 1, GLOSSARY, Mineral and Power Resources, Mineral. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Mineral and Power Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social and Political Life 28 Unit Two 2024-25

The text explains that 29 Teacher’s Note This is a continuation of the theme of government that has already been dealt with in the Class VI and VII textbooks.

The text explains that hence, a recap of ideas is important especially those related to elections, representation and participation.

The text explains that these ideas can be made more clear to students through bringing in actual examples into the classroom.

The text explains that newspaper and TV reports could be used to facilitate this.

The text explains that chapter 3 discusses some of the functions of Parliament.

The text explains that the ways in which these connect to the idea of parliamentary democracy need to be emphasised.

The text explains that hence it is important to explain the critical role played by citizens and allow students to air views regarding this.

The text explains that at times students might be cynical about the political process and your role as a teacher is not to dismiss or agree with this cynicism but rather redirect it towards what the Constitution intends.

The text explains that therefore, they would require more examples from a familiar context.

The text explains that it is through this they can figure out that laws are meant to apply in an equitable manner.

The text explains that the storyboard contained in Chapter 3 portrays how a new law comes into being.

The text explains that the focus of this storyboard is not on the processes within Parliament.

The text explains that in contrast, the storyboard highlights the important role that people play in transforming an urgent social issue into law.

The text explains that in addition to the law already highlighted, it would be good to discuss another example of a new/contemplated law so that students can relate to the role of people in bringing this about.

The text explains that the chapter ends with a section on unpopular laws.

The text explains that these refer to laws that often restrict the Fundamental Rights of certain populations.

The text explains that history provides us with examples of several groups that protest what they view as unjust laws.

The text explains that allow students to research more examples in the Indian context and debate these in the classroom using the Fundamental Rights listed in Chapter 1 as their yardstick.

The text explains that social and Political Life 30 Chapter 3 We in India pride ourselves on being a democracy.

The text explains that here we will try and understand the relation between the ideas of participation in decision-making and the need for all democratic governments to have the consent of their citizens.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will try to see how the Parliament enables citizens of India to participate in decision making and control the government, thus making it the most important symbol of Indian democracy and a key feature of the Constitution.

The text explains that preceding this was a long and difficult struggle in which many sections of society participated.

The text explains that under colonial rule, the people had lived in fear of the British government and did not agree with many of the decisions that they took.

The text explains that the nationalists began to openly criticise the British government and make demands.

The text explains that as far back as 1885, the Indian National Congress demanded that there be elected members in the legislature with a right to discuss the budget and ask questions.

The text explains that the Government of India Act 1909, allowed for some elected representation.

The text explains that while these early legislatures under the British government were in response to the growing demands of the nationalists, they did not allow for all adults to vote nor could people participate in decision making.

The text explains that as you read in Chapter 1, the experience of colonial rule as well as the participation of different people in the struggle for freedom left little doubt in the minds of the nationalists that all persons in independent India would be able to participate in making decisions.

The text explains that this did not mean that the government could do what it felt like, it meant that the government had to be sensitive to people’s needs and demands.

The text explains that the dreams and aspirations of the freedom struggle were made concrete in the Constitution of independent India that laid down the principle of universal adult franchise, i.e. that all adult citizens of the country have the right to vote.

The text explains that chapter 3: Parliament and the Making of Laws 2024-25

The text explains that it is the decision of people that creates a democratic government and decides about its functioning.

The text explains that the basic idea in this kind of democracy is that the individual or the citizen is the most important person and that in principle the government as well as other public institutions need to have the trust of these citizens.

The text explains that how does the individual give approval to the government?

The text explains that people would elect their representatives to the Parliament, then, one group from among these elected representatives forms the government.

The text explains that the Parliament, which is made up of all representatives together, controls and guides the government.

The text explains that in this sense people, through their chosen representatives, form the government and also control it.

The text explains that give one reason why you think there should be universal adult franchise.

The text explains that use the terms ‘constituency’ and ‘represent’ to explain who an MLA is and how the person gets elected?

The text explains that from the list below, identify the work of a State government and that of a Central government.

The text explains that (a) The decision of the Indian government to maintain peaceful relations with China.

The text explains that (b) The decision of the Madhya Pradesh government to discontinue Board exams in Class VIII for all schools under this Board.

The text explains that fill in the blanks with the following words. universal adult franchise; MLAs; representatives; directly Democratic governments in our times are usually referred to as representative democracies.

The text explains that in representative democracies, people do not participate ……...............… but, instead, choose their ….............……..through an election process.

The text explains that sedition: This applies to anything that the government might consider as stirring up resistance or rebellion against it.

The text explains that in such cases, the government does not need absolute evidence in order to arrest persons.

The text explains that under the Sedition Act of 1870, the British had a very broad interpretation of what constituted sedition, and what this meant was that they could arrest and detain any person they wanted under this Act.

The text explains that the nationalists considered this law arbitrary because persons were arrested for a variety of reasons that were seldom clarified beforehand as well as because those arrested were often kept in jail without a trial.

The text explains that repressive: To control severely in order to prevent free and natural development or expression.

The text explains that in the context of this chapter it refers to laws that brutally control persons and often prevent them from exercising their Fundamental Rights including Right to Speech and Assembly.

The text explains that unresolved: Situations in which there are no easy solutions to problems.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 3: Mineral and Power Resources

prelims_traps: Do not treat AJSU Party (AJSU) 1 and GLOSSARY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Mineral and Power Resources; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Mineral and Power Resources through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use AJSU Party (AJSU) 1 and GLOSSARY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
